---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research — Werner L. Hernani-Limarino"
toc: true
toc-title: "Themes"
toc-depth: 2
---

Working papers, submitted manuscripts, and publications organized by research theme. Papers marked *Under review* or *Submitted* are available on SSRN or upon request.

---

## 1. Income Measurement and the Invisible Firm {#income-measurement}

::: {.theme-block}
::: {.theme-desc}
Traditional survey instruments designed for wage workers produce income figures for the self-employed that reverse sign for the majority of informal firms once full costs are accounted for. I develop gold-standard diary validation, ML-based corrections, and structural approaches for contexts where only consumption is observed.
:::

<ul>
<li>
*Ledgers of the Self-Employed: Accounting for the Invisible Firm*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6338941" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
<a href="https://github.com/wernerhl/ledgers-bolivia" target="_blank">[GitHub]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*The Income You Can't See: Detecting and Correcting Self-Employment Measurement Error with Unsupervised Machine Learning*
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Journal of Development Economics*</span>
</li>
<li>
*The Noise Floor: Measurement Error Limits on Poverty Mapping and a Machine Learning Solution*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Recovering Individual Labor Earnings from Household Consumption: A Collective Model Approach with an Application to the Sahel G5*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Identification and Estimation of Income Tails Under Nonclassical Measurement Error: Evidence from Brazil*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
</ul>
:::

---

## 2. Optimal Poverty Targeting {#optimal-targeting}

::: {.theme-block}
::: {.theme-desc}
Proxy means tests and poverty maps break down where poverty populations concentrate — in the distribution tails. I develop augmented ML methods and small area estimation techniques that remain reliable where standard tools fail, validated across 17 LAC countries.
:::

<ul>
<li>
*Optimal Poverty Prediction Under Measurement Error: Theory and Evidence from Augmented Machine Learning*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6332698" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Review & Resubmit, *Journal of Applied Econometrics*</span>
</li>
<li>
*Poverty Mapping in the Tails*
<span class="paper-status">— Submitted, *Journal of Econometrics*</span>
</li>
<li>
*The Tail Test: A Factorial Simulation on the Sources of DRF Advantage in Small Area Estimation*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://github.com/wernerhl/tail-test-replication" target="_blank">[GitHub]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series A*</span>
</li>
<li>
*Data Augmentation for Bias Reduction in Imbalanced Regression*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
</ul>
:::

---

## 3. Distributional Incidence Analysis {#distributional-incidence}

::: {.theme-block}
::: {.theme-desc}
When governments reform subsidies, currencies collapse, or climate shocks hit, who gains and who loses? I develop real-time distributional monitoring tools combining microsimulation, ML filters, and minimum cross-entropy reweighting to estimate welfare impacts between survey rounds.
:::

<ul>
<li>
*Between Surveys: Real-Time Distributional Monitoring for Vulnerable Populations*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Forecasting the Distributional Impacts of Climate Change and Climate Policy*
<span class="paper-status">— World Bank Technical Note</span>
</li>
<li>
*Who Bears the Cost of Devaluation? Price Dynamics and Welfare Impacts During Bolivia's 2024–2025 Currency Crisis*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://github.com/wernerhl/BOL-NoReturns" target="_blank">[GitHub]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Latin American Journal of Development*</span>
</li>
<li>
*When the Pump Becomes a Shock: Stress-Adjusted Incidence of Fuel Subsidy Reform in a Fragile Informal Economy*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Estimands, Specification Sensitivity, and Tail Reliability in Growth Incidence Curve Analysis*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Measuring Redistribution Effects of Climate Change*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
</ul>
:::

---

## 4. Structural Poverty, Informality, and Inclusion Policy {#structural-poverty}

::: {.theme-block}
::: {.theme-desc}
Not all poverty is the same. I distinguish workers structurally trapped in loss-making informal firms by accumulated relational capital from the transitory poor, and derive the distinct policy implications for social inclusion, social investment, and social protection.
:::

<ul>
<li>
*Structural Poverty in Latin America: Definition, Measurement, and Twenty Years of Change*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*The Three Faces of Inequality: Systematic, Structural, and Residual Inequality in Latin America, 2000–2022*
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Journal of Development Economics*</span>
</li>
<li>
*Trapped by Cash: A Theory of Voluntary Informality Among the Economically Non-Viable*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6338941" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*To Be a Taquero or to Build a Taquería: Occupational Sorting under Risk and Embedded Capital*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6332698" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Journal of Development Economics*</span>
</li>
</ul>
:::

---

## 5. Intergenerational Mobility and Inequality of Opportunity {#mobility}

::: {.theme-block}
::: {.theme-desc}
Falling intergenerational coefficients across LAC mask rising positional rigidity. I develop the Mobility Frontier framework and the schooling lag methodology, and apply both to Mexico, Bolivia, and 18 LAC countries using census and survey data spanning five decades.
:::

<ul>
<li>
*The Mobility Frontier: Why the Intergenerational Elasticity Is an Insufficient Statistic*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Sticky Ends, Mobile Middle: The Shape of Intergenerational Persistence in Mexico*
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Journal of Economic Inequality*</span>
</li>
<li>
*The Schooling Lag: Rank Persistence and the Measurement of Intergenerational Educational Mobility*
<span class="paper-status">— Submitted, *Review of Income and Wealth*</span>
</li>
<li>
*Mexico's Mobility Mirage: Falling Intergenerational Coefficients, Rising Positional Rigidity*
<span class="paper-status">— Target: *Journal of Development Economics*</span>
</li>
<li>
*Between Communities and Within Them: A Five-Census Decomposition of Intergenerational Persistence in Bolivia, 1976–2024*
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Network Thickness, Inequality of Opportunity, and the Persistence of Advantage*
<span class="paper-status">— Submitted, *Social Choice and Welfare*</span>
</li>
</ul>
:::

---

## 6. Gender, Adaptive Preferences, and Social Norms {#gender}

::: {.theme-block}
::: {.theme-desc}
Persistent gender inequality operates through preferences and norms, not just endowments and returns. When preferences are endogenous to the distribution, redistribution alone cannot close gaps. I develop the theory and provide intersectional evidence across LAC.
:::

<ul>
<li>
*Adaptive Preferences, Endogenous Returns, and the Failure of the Second Welfare Theorem*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6338941" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Mainstreaming the Wrong Channel: Gender Policy, Adaptive Preferences, and the Limits of Redistribution*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6338941" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
<li>
*Compounding Penalties: Gender, Ethnicity, and the Structure of Wage Inequality Among Indigenous Women in Latin America*
<span class="paper-links">
<a href="https://ssrn.com/abstract=6338941" target="_blank">[SSRN]</a>
</span>
<span class="paper-status">— Working Paper</span>
</li>
</ul>
:::

---

## Earlier Publications (Selected)

<ul class="pub-list">
<li>
*Meta-analysis of ILO interventions in Latin America and the Caribbean on child labor, forced labor and human trafficking.*
<span class="pub-source">Organización Internacional del Trabajo, 2020.</span>
</li>
<li>
*Intended and Unintended Effects of Non-contributory Pensions: The Case of Bolivia's Renta Dignidad* (with G. Mena).
<span class="pub-source">IDB Working Paper Series No. IDB-WP-631, 2015.</span>
</li>
<li>
*Are Teachers Well Paid in Latin America and the Caribbean?* In *Incentives to Improve Teaching: Lessons from Latin America*, E. Vegas (Ed.).
<span class="pub-source">World Bank, 2005.</span>
</li>
<li>
*Regional Integration and Wage Inequality* (with S. Duryea and R. Robertson). In *Beyond Borders: 2002 Report on Economic and Social Progress in Latin America*.
<span class="pub-source">Inter-American Development Bank, 2002.</span>
</li>
<li>
*Bolivia: Efectos de la Liberalización sobre el Crecimiento, Empleo, Distribución del Ingreso y Pobreza* (with R. Pereira and W. Jiménez). In *Liberalización, Desigualdad y Pobreza: América Latina y el Caribe en los 90*.
<span class="pub-source">UNDP, 2002.</span>
</li>
<li>
*La evolución de la pobreza en Bolivia: un enfoque multidimensional* (with P. Villarroel).
<span class="pub-source">*Revista Latinoamericana de Desarrollo Económico*, No. 20, 2013.</span>
</li>
<li>
*Unemployment in Bolivia: Risks and Labor Market Policies* (with E. Yáñez and M.A. Villegas).
<span class="pub-source">IDB Working Paper Series No. IDB-WP-271.</span>
</li>
</ul>
